Foundation Model Embeddings for Multi-Omics Cancer Classification
Integrating BulkFormer and CpGPT with Fusion Strategies for TCGA Pan-Cancer Analysis
Mila Todorovska  ·  Bojana Andonova  ·  Sandra
Faculty of Computer Science and Engineering · Ss. Cyril and Methodius University · 2024
800Patients
6Cancer Types
5Experiments
97.7%Best Accuracy
0.999Best AUC-ROC
Abstract

Cancer type classification from multi-omics data is a critical challenge in precision oncology. This study evaluates two genomic foundation models — BulkFormer (RNA-seq) and CpGPT (DNA methylation) — for classifying six TCGA cancer types: BRCA, LUAD, COAD, KIRC, LIHC, and THCA (n=800 patients). We compare single-modality embeddings against three multi-omics fusion strategies (Early, Late, and Deep Fusion via cross-modal autoencoder) and a classical MOFA+ baseline. BulkFormer achieves 97.7% accuracy (AUC=0.999) using only 1,152 dimensions versus 60,616 raw genes — a 52× compression with less than 2% accuracy loss. CpGPT achieves 89.0% with 128 dimensions. All fusion models exceed 96% accuracy with AUC=0.999. KEGG pathway enrichment links discriminative CpG probes to Wnt signaling, PI3K-Akt, and cell cycle regulation — established cancer driver mechanisms. Our results demonstrate that foundation model embeddings provide compact, biologically meaningful representations competitive with raw omics data.

Keywords: foundation models, multi-omics, cancer classification, BulkFormer, CpGPT, TCGA, methylation, RNA-seq, fusion strategies, MOFA+, KEGG, XGBoost

1. Introduction

1.1 Motivation

Cancer classification from multi-omics data enables personalized treatment decisions. Traditional machine learning methods require large feature sets (tens of thousands of genes or CpG probes), limiting interpretability and generalization. Foundation models trained on large biological datasets offer compact, transferable representations that may match or outperform raw-feature approaches.

1.2 Research Questions

RQ1: Can foundation model embeddings classify cancer types with high accuracy?
RQ2: Are multi-omics fusion strategies superior to single-modality approaches?
RQ3: Do foundation model representations encode biologically meaningful signals?
RQ4: How do foundation models compare to classical methods such as MOFA+?

1.3 Dataset

We use TCGA level-3 data for 800 matched patients across 6 cancer types (~133 samples per type): BRCA (breast), LUAD (lung), COAD (colon), KIRC (kidney), LIHC (liver), THCA (thyroid). All samples have both RNA-seq (60,616 genes) and methylation array (49,156 CpG probes) measurements.

2. Methods

2.1 Foundation Models

BulkFormer [Theodoris et al., 2023] is a transformer pretrained on large-scale bulk transcriptomics datasets. We extract the mean-pooled final hidden state (1,152-dim) as the RNA embedding. Genes are rank-ordered by expression within each sample before tokenization.

CpGPT [Fang et al., 2024] is a transformer trained on methylation arrays. We use the CLS token (128-dim) as the methylation embedding, selecting the top-2,048 most variable CpG probes as input tokens.

2.2 Fusion Strategies

Early Fusion: Embeddings are concatenated (1,280-dim) and fed directly to XGBoost.

Late Fusion: Separate XGBoost classifiers per modality; class probabilities averaged at inference.

Deep Fusion: A cross-modal autoencoder learns a 64-dim shared latent space from both embeddings, trained with modality-specific reconstruction losses (AdamW, 200 epochs, CosineAnnealingLR). The latent space is used as classifier input.

2.3 Classical Baseline — MOFA+

Multi-Omics Factor Analysis (MOFA+) with 30 factors is trained strictly within each CV fold (top-5,000 methylation + top-2,000 RNA features). MOFA+ factors serve as XGBoost input, providing a rigorous classical multi-omics comparison.

2.4 Classification & Evaluation

XGBoost classifier (n_estimators=300, max_depth=6, learning_rate=0.05) with 5-fold StratifiedKFold cross-validation. Metrics: Accuracy, macro F1, macro AUC-ROC (reported as mean ± std across folds). SHAP TreeExplainer provides feature importance for the best-performing model.

Deep Fusion (Autoencoder)0.966 ± 0.0080.966 ± 0.0090.999 ± 0.001Fusion

Table 1. Full results (5-fold CV, mean ± std). Green highlight = best foundation model.

3.1 Key Findings

BulkFormer (97.7%) matches raw RNA (99.5%) within 1.8% using 52× fewer dimensions. CpGPT (89.0%) is 10% below raw methylation (99.0%) — stronger compression loss. All fusion strategies reach AUC=0.999, identical to BulkFormer alone, indicating the discriminative ceiling is reached by RNA embeddings for this 6-class task. MOFA+ (97.6%) is virtually identical to BulkFormer (97.7%), validating both approaches.

3.2 UMAP Embedding Visualizations

3.3 Feature Importance (SHAP)

3.4 KEGG Pathway Enrichment Analysis

ANOVA F-statistics across 49,156 CpG probes identify the 500 most discriminative sites. These probes are mapped to gene names via Illumina 450K annotation and submitted to KEGG enrichment via Enrichr. The figure below shows the top 20 enriched pathways.

4. Discussion

4.1 Foundation Models vs. Raw Features

BulkFormer achieves within 1.8% of raw RNA performance while using 52× fewer dimensions. This confirms that pretraining on large-scale bulk transcriptomes encodes tissue-discriminative signals transferable to cancer classification without task-specific fine-tuning. CpGPT's larger gap (10%) reflects stronger information compression: distilling 49,156 probes into 128 dimensions discards more discriminative information than BulkFormer's 1,152-dim representation.

4.2 Fusion Strategy Analysis

Early, Late, and Deep Fusion all perform comparably to BulkFormer alone (~97%), suggesting that the weaker CpGPT signal does not substantially complement the already strong RNA representation. Fusion strategies would likely show greater benefit for harder tasks — e.g., intra-cancer subtype classification (BRCA luminal A vs. B) where single-modality performance is lower and both omics provide complementary signals. The Deep Fusion autoencoder (96.6%, 64-dim) offers the most compact multi-omics representation, useful for downstream visualization and interpretation.

4.3 Classical Baseline Comparison

MOFA+ (97.6%) virtually matches BulkFormer (97.7%), validating both approaches. However, MOFA+ requires fitting a matrix factorization model inside each CV fold and is sensitive to factor count selection. Foundation model embeddings are pre-computed once and reused across all experiments, making them substantially more scalable and deployable.

4.4 Biological Interpretation

KEGG enrichment reveals complement and coagulation cascades (liver-specific, consistent with LIHC dominance), thyroid hormone synthesis (THCA-specific), and metabolic reprogramming pathways (consistent with known cancer metabolic alterations). SHAP analysis confirms that a small subset of BulkFormer embedding dimensions drives classification, suggesting concentrated, interpretable biological signal rather than diffuse information.

5. Conclusion

Key Findings

  • BulkFormer achieves 97.7% accuracy — within 1.8% of raw RNA features using 52× fewer dimensions
  • All methods reach AUC=0.999, confirming strong separability of these 6 cancer types from omics data
  • Fusion strategies do not substantially improve over BulkFormer alone for this task
  • MOFA+ (97.6%) matches foundation models, validating both the classical and deep learning approaches
  • KEGG enrichment links discriminative probes to known cancer-driver pathways (Wnt, PI3K-Akt, thyroid signaling)
  • Foundation models provide compact, biologically meaningful representations enabling efficient multi-omics integration without task-specific training

References

[1] Dalla-Torre, H. et al. (2023). BulkFormer: a foundation model for bulk transcriptomics. bioRxiv.
[2] Fang, Z. et al. (2024). CpGPT: a foundation model for the DNA methylome. bioRxiv.
[3] Argelaguet, R. et al. (2020). MOFA+: a statistical framework for comprehensive integration of multi-modal single-cell data. Genome Biology, 21(1), 111.
[4] TCGA Research Network (2013). The Cancer Genome Atlas Pan-Cancer analysis project. Nature Genetics, 45, 1113–1120.
[5] Chen, T. & Guestrin, C. (2016). XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System. KDD '16, 785–794.
[6] Lundberg, S.M. & Lee, S.I. (2017). A unified approach to interpreting model predictions. NeurIPS.
[7] Kanehisa, M. & Goto, S. (2000). KEGG: Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes. Nucleic Acids Research, 28(1), 27–30.
